talk_title: AI-augmented drug discovery across biologics, small molecules, and multimodal biomarkers

biomarkers.name: membrane-to-cytoplasm optical density difference
biomarkers.finding: correlates with internalization rate of datupizumab duoxetecane and predicts response in Trop‑2 ADC trials

models.name: AstraZeneca‑Tempus‑Pathos multimodal model
models.genotype_or_key_features: longitudinal EHR, labs, H&E, genomics, transcriptomics

MOA_summary: AstraZeneca embeds AI models to predict protein structures for de novo mini‑protein binders (17% hit rate, 28 nM affinity) and to guide small‑molecule optimization (potency, clearance, exposure) for an intractable target, while computational pathology AI quantifies membrane‑cytoplasm optical density to predict ADC internalization, and multimodal foundation models integrate EHR, imaging, and omics to improve patient selection and trial design.
key_findings: AI‑designed mini‑protein library yielded 45 binders (17% success) with highest affinity of 28 nM in just one month, a three‑fold speedup over traditional methods.

Reinvent AI tool accelerated small‑molecule lead optimization for a confidential target, reducing the design cycle from ~8 months to ~4 months while achieving target‑level potency, clearance, exposure, and tumor growth inhibition.

Computational pathology model identified membrane‑to‑cytoplasm optical density difference as a surrogate for internalization of the TROP2 ADC datupizumab duoxetecane, now entering prospective validation in a phase‑III trial.

A multimodal foundation model built with Tempus and Pathos integrates longitudinal EHR, labs, histology, and genomics to predict control‑arm performance and patient response, aiming to improve phase‑III success rates.
open_questions: How can AI‑driven computational pathology be scaled across diverse ADC platforms beyond TROP2?
What are the optimal data governance frameworks to share multimodal clinical data while preserving patient privacy?

talk_title: Chromatin remodeling complexes as AI‑guided therapeutic targets

speaker.name: Sigal Kadish
speaker.affiliation: Harvard University, Professor of Molecular Biology; Co‑founder, Foghorn Therapeutics

targets.dependency_context: various solid tumors with ARID1A loss
BAF complex subunits across >1,000 cancer cell lines

models.name: pan‑cancer dependency screen
models.genotype_or_key_features: 1,000+ cancer cell lines, BAF complex knockouts

MOA_summary: AI‑driven AlphaFold‑based modeling identified a novel immunoglobulin‑like SWIFT domain within BAF chromatin remodelers that mediates transcription‑factor interactions; hydrogen‑deuterium exchange validated TF‑SWIFT binding sites, and CRISPR dependency screens revealed synthetic lethalities (e.g., SMARCA2 dependence in SMARCA4‑mutant lung cancer), establishing these interfaces as emerging therapeutic targets.
key_findings: AI‑predicted SWIFT domain structures correctly identified three PU.1 TAD binding motifs, validated by pull‑down and HDX experiments.
Fitness correlation across >1,000 cancer cell lines highlighted SWIFT‑containing paralogs (SMARCD1‑3) as top codependencies, suggesting selective vulnerability.
Histone modification profiling showed that specific nucleosome acetylation patterns dramatically modulate BAF complex remodeling activity, revealing allele‑specific inhibition opportunities.
Synthetic‑lethal relationship between SMARCA2 and SMARCA4 mutations was confirmed, positioning SMARCA2 as a high‑value target in SMARCA4‑mutant lung cancer.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Paralog redundancy within BAF complexes may confer resistance; targeting SWIFT domain offers paralog‑specific selectivity.

combination_strategies.rationale: BAF complex loss induces replication stress, sensitizing cells to ATR blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor regression in SMARCA4‑mutant xenografts

open_questions: Can AI‑designed small molecules achieve sufficient selectivity for SWIFT‑mediated PPIs without off‑target chromatin effects?
What is the therapeutic window for targeting intrinsically disordered regions of BAF subunits?

talk_title: AI‑driven de novo antibody design with atomic precision

models.name: internal high‑resolution folding model (AlphaFold‑derived)
models.genotype_or_key_features: atomic‑level predictions for antibodies and antigens

models.name: epitope selection agent
models.genotype_or_key_features: guides design to avoid off‑target binding

clinical_data.value: KRAS G12V mutant binding, no WT binding

MOA_summary: Chai Discovery uses diffusion‑based generative models and high‑resolution folding predictions to design full‑length antibodies (including VHH) that directly target defined epitopes on GPCRs, peptide‑MHC complexes, and cytokine interfaces, achieving nanomolar affinities and functional agonism/antagonism without library screening, while agentic AI enforces developability constraints and off‑target avoidance.
key_findings: Design of antibodies against >50 diverse targets succeeded with ≤20 designs per target, yielding binders for ~50% of targets (≈10‑fold improvement over prior 0.1% rates).
Atomic‑resolution predictions matched cryo‑EM structures (global RMSD <2 Å, CDR loop RMSD <1 Å) for five test cases, confirming near‑perfect epitope modeling.
GPCR VHH agonist for CCR8 achieved functional signaling after testing only 10 designs, with 1 agonist hit, demonstrating feasibility of de novo agonist design.
Peptide‑MHC antibody selectively bound KRAS G12V peptide‑MHC complex while sparing WT KRAS and alternative MHCs, illustrating precise epitope discrimination.
Developability metrics (expression, stability, viscosity, polyreactivity) of AI‑generated antibodies matched or exceeded those of clinically approved mAbs on first‑pass designs.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Model failures traced to missing constraints (e.g., membrane or glycan clashes), highlighting need for explicit negative selection prompts.

combination_strategies.rationale: GPCR agonist antibodies may enhance T‑cell infiltration when combined with checkpoint blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced tumor regression in murine models

open_questions: How can AI agents be further trained to predict and avoid immunogenic epitopes during antibody design?
What is the upper limit of design‑to‑experiment cycles before diminishing returns in hit rate?
